name: Umm Haram bint Milhan
title: Umm Haram bint Milhan (RA): The Martyr of the Sea
meta_description: The story of Umm Haram bint Milhan (RA), the aunt who combed the Prophet's hair as he slept, asked to be among a people she had never seen, and died at sea decades later, exactly as promised.
keywords: Umm Haram bint Milhan, story of Umm Haram, martyr of the sea, aunt of the Prophet, sahaba stories, the companions, women companions
lede: She was sitting in her own home in Madinah, far from any coast, among people who had never ridden a ship. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ fell asleep while she combed his hair, then woke up smiling. He told her he had seen a people of his community sailing the sea like kings. She asked him to pray that she would be one of them.

This is the story of Umm Haram bint Milhan (may Allah be pleased with her): a woman who lost almost everyone she loved, kept her composure when others wailed, and held on to one quiet request until the day it carried her across the water to her death.

sections.h2: A family that lived for one word
paras: Umm Haram belonged to a remarkable household among the Ansar of Madinah. She was the sister of Umm Sulaim, and the aunt of the young servant of the Prophet ﷺ, Anas ibn Malik. Her people were known for a particular kind of love for the Prophet ﷺ: they always wanted to be near him, always looking for something to give.

Across the whole family the same theme keeps repeating. A son dedicated to serve the Prophet ﷺ. A garden given away the moment a verse called for it. An uncle who missed the Battle of Badr, grieved that he had not been at the Prophet's side in danger, and ran toward the next battle when everyone else ran from it. Each of them, in their own way, lived for one word: "Ya Allah, accept it from me."

Their love for the Prophet ﷺ was not the quiet kind. It was always reaching for more, always wanting to give and to be near him, even at cost. Out of that household came one promise after another, made to Allah and then kept, sometimes within hours and sometimes only after long years of waiting.

Umm Haram does not seem to have had a son named Haram. She was named after her younger brother, just as her sister was named after hers, because both women were like mothers to their siblings. It is a small detail, but it tells you what kind of woman she was before the rest of the story even begins.

sections.h2: The day she lost her husband and her son
paras: When Islam reached her home, Umm Haram accepted it together with her husband, Amr ibn Qays, and her older son, Qays. Both men were early and wholehearted. Both fought at Badr and became veterans of that day. She also had a baby boy, Abdullah, born after they entered Islam.
Then came the Battle of Uhud, and it broke something in nearly every house in Madinah. Few families lost both a husband and a son in a single day. Umm Haram was one of those few.

She walked toward the battlefield as the men were leaving it, weeping, more than seventy of their own lying dead. She asked, "What happened to my husband?" A man stopped and told her Amr had died as a martyr. She lowered her head and said, "Truly, to Allah we belong and to Him we return." Then she kept walking and asked about her son. He too had been killed. Again she said the same words, sat down, and grieved, but she did not break.

To understand how unusual her response was, you have to know the custom of the time. When a death was announced, people would scream, strike their cheeks, and compete in loud, public mourning. That practice was only forbidden after Uhud. On that day it was still normal.

So when this woman, having just learned in the space of minutes that both her husband and her older son were gone, answered only with "Truly, to Allah we belong and to Him we return," the other women noticed. Her composure was remembered and spoken about. She was left with a baby and a fresh, double grief, and still she carried herself with a stillness that stood out among everyone around her.

It is worth pausing on this. These were not people who felt loss any less than we do. They wept, they grieved, they buried the ones they loved. What set her apart was not the absence of pain but where she turned with it. Her first words were not a cry against fate but a return to Allah, spoken before the practice of loud mourning had even been forbidden.
And this was only the beginning of her loss.

Her younger brother, Haram ibn Milhan, was among the earliest of the family to accept Islam, even before her. He was one of those who had memorised what had been revealed of the Quran. He studied it by day and stood with it in prayer at night, part of a group of devoted young men who carried water and brought food to the poor in the mosque. The Prophet ﷺ loved them.

Four months after Uhud, a delegation came asking the Prophet ﷺ to send reciters to teach them the Quran. He sent about seventy of his best companions. At a place called Bir Maunah they were ambushed and killed, almost the same number who had fallen at Uhud. Haram was among them.

As he was struck down, he looked up and said, "By the Lord of the Kaaba, I have succeeded." Even the man who killed him was bewildered by the words. For Umm Haram, it meant that within a single span of four months she had lost her husband, her older son, and the brother who was like a son to her.

After her waiting period ended, a man of great standing and noble character, Ubadah ibn al-Samit, asked to marry her, and she accepted. She would later describe him by one thing above all: he raised her young son Abdullah as if he were his own. The nobility of these companions, she understood, was not only shown on a battlefield. It was shown at home.

Because she and her sister were counted as aunts to the Prophet ﷺ, and because their family had given so much, he used to visit their homes, eat with them, and rest there. One day he came and prayed a voluntary prayer with the small household, just to bless them. Anas, still a boy, prayed beside him while his mother and his aunt prayed behind.

On another visit Umm Haram fed him, then sat behind him and combed through his hair. Her touch was gentle, and he fell asleep. When he woke, he was smiling. She asked, "What is making you laugh, Messenger of Allah?" He said he had been shown a people of his community going out in the path of Allah, riding the sea like kings upon their thrones. These were desert people who had never seen a ship. Without a thought for how it could ever happen, she said, "Pray that Allah makes me one of them." He prayed for her and said, "You are among them."

ayah.text: There are men among the believers who honoured their pledge to God: some of them have fulfilled it by death, and some are still waiting. They have not changed in the least.

paras: She carried that promise for the rest of her life. Somehow, she knew, she would end up on the water. Her husband Ubadah rose to lead armies, and under the early caliphs he was placed in charge of one land after another, living for a time in Palestine and then beyond. The Muslims had no navy, and for years a fleet was thought too dangerous to launch, even as attacks kept coming against them from across the sea. Eventually permission was given, and Ubadah was put in command of the community's first expedition by sea, toward Cyprus.

Umm Haram told him plainly that she was coming with him. She was about seventy-five years old. He did not argue, and she boarded the ship and crossed the sea. The one well-known narration she passed down happens to be about exactly this: that whoever falls ill at sea in the path of Allah has the reward of a martyr, and whoever drowns has the reward of two.

When the ships reached land and the doors opened, she came off riding her horse, just as the soldiers brought their animals ashore. As soon as the horse touched the ground she fell, and she died at once. She was buried there, the only companion laid to rest on that island, and she became known forever as the Martyr of the Sea.

Think of the distance between the two moments. A woman sitting in a simple home in the desert, among people who had never seen the open water, hearing a sleeping Prophet ﷺ wake with a smile and describe a sea she would never have imagined. And then, decades later, an old woman crossing that very sea, falling at the edge of a far island, her single request answered to the letter. The scholars say the Prophet ﷺ guaranteed her sister Paradise, and guaranteed Umm Haram both Paradise and martyrdom.

lessons_heading: What Umm Haram's life teaches us
lessons_intro: Her story is not loud. It is a woman who grieved more than most, asked Allah for one thing, and waited years without ever doubting that it would come.

lessons.bold: Ask, and leave the how to Allah.
lessons.text: She heard of a people sailing the sea and did not say "but I am only a woman" or "I will be old by then." She asked to be among them, and trusted Allah with the rest.

lessons.bold: A sincere intention is itself a deed.
lessons.text: She held one quiet request for decades. The lesson her whole family teaches is to mean what you ask Allah for, and not to worry about whether you can see the path.

lessons.bold: Patience can be the loudest witness.
lessons.text: While others wailed, she answered her grief with a single verse. Her composure said more about her faith than any words could have.
She praised her husband not for his victories but because he raised her orphaned son as his own. The truest nobility is the kind no crowd ever sees.

faq.q: Who was Umm Haram bint Milhan?
faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the Ansar of Madinah, the sister of Umm Sulaim and an aunt of Anas ibn Malik. She is remembered as the Martyr of the Sea after she died on the community's first naval expedition, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had foretold.

faq.q: Why is she called the Martyr of the Sea?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ once saw in a dream a people of his community sailing the sea in the path of Allah, and he told her she would be among them. Many years later she joined the first naval expedition toward Cyprus, and when the ships reached land she fell from her horse and died and was buried on the island.

faq.q: What was the dream the Prophet ﷺ saw?
faq.a: He fell asleep while she combed his hair and woke smiling. He said he had been shown believers going out by sea in the path of Allah, like kings upon their thrones. She asked him to pray that she be one of them, and he told her she was.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Umm Haram?
faq.a: To make a sincere request of Allah and leave the means to Him, to meet loss with patience rather than despair, and to remember that the noblest character is shown quietly, at home, where no crowd is watching.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us never carry a single hope for forty years. We ask, and when nothing changes quickly we quietly let it go. Umm Haram heard one promise in her own living room and held it through old age, through more grief than most people meet in a lifetime, certain that Allah would bring it about in His time.

Her life is an invitation to ask Allah for the thing you would love most, sincerely, and then to stop measuring how far away it looks. Meet your losses the way she met hers: with a steady heart and a single honest sentence. And when you pray today, name what you truly want, and trust the One who heard a request she made while combing a sleeping man's hair, and answered it on the far side of the sea.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Umm Haram bint Milhan (RA) and her family in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 33:23). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
She was sitting at home in Madinah, far from any sea.

The Prophet ﷺ fell asleep as she combed his hair, then woke up smiling.

What he told her would shape the rest of her life.

First came the losses.

At the Battle of Uhud she lost her husband and her older son on the same day.

Few families lost both.

When she heard, she did not wail like the others around her.

She lowered her head and said only:

"Truly, to Allah we belong and to Him we return."

Four months later, her younger brother was killed too.

As he fell he said, **"By the Lord of the Kaaba, I have succeeded."**

Three of the people she loved, gone in one season.

The Prophet ﷺ woke from his nap smiling.

He had seen believers sailing the sea in the path of Allah, like kings upon their thrones.

Desert people who had never seen a ship.

She did not say "but I am only a woman."

She did not say "I will be old by then."

She said: **"Pray that Allah makes me one of them."**

He said: "You are among them."

She carried that promise for decades.

At about seventy-five, she boarded the community's first ship to cross the sea.

When it reached land, she fell from her horse and died at once.

**Ask Allah, and leave the how to Him.**

She held one quiet hope for forty years, and it carried her across the water in the end.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
Umm Haram bint Milhan (may Allah be pleased with her) was sitting in her home in Madinah, among people who had never seen the sea, when the Prophet ﷺ fell asleep as she combed his hair and woke up smiling. He had been shown believers sailing in the path of Allah, like kings upon their thrones.

She asked one thing: that she be among them. She had already lost her husband, her son, and her brother, and met all of it with patience. She held that single hope for decades, until the community's first naval expedition carried her across the water, where she died and was buried, exactly as foretold.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/umm-haram

email.subject: She asked for something she could not yet imagine
email.alt_subject: The dream the Prophet ﷺ saw while she combed his hair
email.preview: She held one quiet request for decades, and it carried her across the sea.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Umm Haram bint Milhan (may Allah be pleased with her) lived in Madinah, far from any coast, among people who had never ridden a ship.

Her life held more loss than most. At the Battle of Uhud she lost her husband and her older son on the same day. Few families lost both. When she was told, she did not wail the way others did. She lowered her head and said only, "Truly, to Allah we belong and to Him we return." Four months later her younger brother was killed as well. Three of the people she loved most, gone in a single season.

Then one day the Prophet ﷺ came to her home. She fed him, and sat behind him, and combed through his hair until he fell asleep. When he woke, he was smiling. She asked him why.

He said he had been shown a people of his community going out in the path of Allah, sailing the sea like kings upon their thrones. These were desert people. They had never seen a ship.

She did not ask how it could happen. She did not say she was only a woman, or that she would be old by then. She said, "Pray that Allah makes me one of them." He prayed for her, and told her, "You are among them."

She carried that one promise for the rest of her life. Years passed. Her husband came to lead the community's first expedition by sea, and at about seventy-five years old she told him plainly that she was coming too. She crossed the water with the fleet. When the ships reached land, she came ashore on her horse, fell, and died at once. She was buried there, the only companion laid to rest on that island, and she is remembered to this day as the Martyr of the Sea.

There is a lesson in her that is easy to miss. Ask Allah for the thing you would love most, sincerely, and leave the how to Him. She held one hope for decades without ever doubting it, and it carried her exactly where she had asked to go.

May Allah be pleased with her, and grant us sincere hearts and patient ones.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Umm Haram bint Milhan (may Allah be pleased with her) lived in Madinah, in a family known for their love of the Prophet ﷺ. They were always near him, always looking for something to give. Each of them seemed to live for one quiet word: Ya Allah, accept it from me.

Her life held great loss. At the Battle of Uhud she lost her husband and her older son on the same day, and few families lost both. When she heard the news, she did not wail the way the people around her did. She lowered her head and said only, "Truly, to Allah we belong and to Him we return." Her composure was so unusual that the other women remembered it.

Then, four months later, her younger brother was killed too, a young man who had memorised the Quran and was like a son to her. As he fell he looked up and said, "By the Lord of the Kaaba, I have succeeded." In a single season she had lost three of the people she loved most.

In time she married a noble man, Ubadah ibn al-Samit, and she loved him most of all for one thing: he raised her young son as if he were his own. The Prophet ﷺ, who counted her among his aunts, would visit her home, eat there, and rest there.

One day she fed him, then sat behind him and gently combed his hair until he fell asleep. When he woke, he was smiling. She asked him why. He said he had been shown believers sailing the sea in the path of Allah, like kings upon their thrones. They were desert people who had never seen a ship.
She did not ask how such a thing could ever happen to her. She simply said, "Pray that Allah makes me one of them." He prayed for her, and told her, "You are among them."

She carried that promise for decades. When her husband came to lead the community's first expedition by sea, she was about seventy-five, and she told him plainly that she was coming too. She crossed the water with the fleet. When the ships reached land and she came ashore on her horse, she fell, and she died at once. She was buried on that island, the only companion to rest there, and she is remembered to this day as the Martyr of the Sea.
storybook.pullquote: Pray that Allah makes me one of them.
storybook.reflection: Umm Haram heard one promise in her own living room and held it for the rest of her life, through more grief than most people ever meet. She did not worry about how it could happen, or whether she was the right person for it. She simply asked, sincerely, and waited. Her life invites us to name the thing we most want from Allah, to ask Him for it with a whole heart, and then to leave the how to the One who answered a request she made while combing a sleeping man's hair.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
